Author byline on substantive content (Experience + Expertise)
Author credentials visible (Authoritativeness)
Practising certificate disclosure
Honest claims (no superlatives without substantiation)
Pricing transparency (incl. VAT or excluding clearly stated)
Honest reviews disclosure (no fake or paid reviews)
All-in pricing, no hidden mandatory fees (DMCC drip-pricing ban)
UK limited companies must display registered name, number, registered office on every website.
Consumer Rights Act 2015 requires clear rights notice on commercial websites.
Total price including mandatory fees must be displayed up-front, not added at checkout.
Must publish a policy declaring no incentivised, fake or undisclosed reviews.
Reviews or testimonials shown without disclosing incentives or a genuine-review verification process
Clear subscription terms + easy cancellation (DMCC)
Section 3 personal data definition disclosure
Comparison claims substantiated
Representative APR + credit risk warnings (FCA CONC)
Fair, clear, not-misleading financial promotion (FCA COBS 4)
FSCS exclusion disclosure (eMoney not covered)
Transparency report for public-interest audits
Risk warning on investment/financial promotions
Section 21 approval / authorised-person status for promotions
Legitimate interests (if relied on) clearly stated
Recipients or categories of recipients of personal data
Whether provision is statutory/contractual and consequences of not providing
Existence of automated decision-making including profiling
Cookie purposes must be itemised (functional vs analytics vs marketing)
Cookie list must include third-party cookies
Cookie banner copy must be available before clicking accept
Cookie consent log must be retained for 12 months minimum
Re-prompt for consent after material changes to cookie usage
Commercial organisations turnover >£36M must publish annual Modern Slavery Act statement.
Marketing or analytics trackers present but no consent-management platform gating them before consent
Cookie banner must offer a Reject-All / non-essential-refusal option as prominent and functional as Accept-All
B2B corporate subscriber: soft opt-in still required for individual employees identifiable in role
Cookie withdrawal mechanism accessible from every page
Senior Managers and Certification Regime reference
FCA-regulated firms must publish senior-manager accountability statements + statements of responsibility.
Unit pricing for pre-packaged goods
ICT risk-management + third-party register (DORA)
Text alternatives for non-text content (WCAG 1.1.1)
Full keyboard operability (WCAG 2.1.1)
Right to rectification
Right to restriction of processing
Automated decision-making + profiling safeguards
Records of processing activities (ROPA)
Personal data breach notification within 72 hours
Communication of a breach to data subjects
Data protection impact assessment (DPIA)
Data Protection Officer appointment + contact
Fair, clear, not-misleading investment communications (MiFID II)
Incident-reporting + security-measures posture (NIS2)
No greenwashing in financial product disclosures (SFDR)
Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)
Published content shows author / expertise signals (E-E-A-T)
Code of Ethics reference
Substantiated, non-misleading environmental/green claims
No false urgency / misleading countdown timers
Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website
VAT-registered businesses must show VAT number on website per HMRC guidance.
Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
Fair, transparent and legible consumer contract terms
Age-appropriate design (ICO Children Code)
Section 22 manual unstructured data exemption noted where applicable
Image alt text as a reasonable adjustment
UK right of access (Subject Access Request)
UK right to erasure
UK right to data portability
UK right to object to direct marketing
UK processor / sub-processor transparency
UK records of processing (ROPA)
UK lawful basis identified
Cookie scan must distinguish session vs persistent cookies
Cookie banner not shown to authenticated users with stored preferences
Valid marketing consent or soft opt-in for email/SMS
Strictly necessary cookies do not require consent but must be disclosed
Distance-selling pre-contract information (CCRs 2013)
Customer due-diligence / KYC posture (6AMLD)

Payment refund + dispute rights disclosed (PSD2)
Strong Customer Authentication + transparent payment terms (PSD2)
Internal whistleblowing channel (EU Whistleblower Directive)
ACCA/ICAEW regulated-status + non-misleading accountancy claims
VAT number displayed where VAT-registered

FOS + FSCS protection disclosure for financial services

Modern Slavery statement (turnover GBP 36M+)
FSCS deposit-protection disclosure (PRA)
Senior-manager accountability for published content (SM&CR)
Clear price marking incl. unit/total price

terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.

exec_summary: The most serious regulatory risk is the potential GBP 103,150,000 fine exposure due to multiple P0 findings, which matters because it poses a significant financial threat to the organisation. Resolving these critical issues, particularly around cookies, data processing, and disclosure, presents an opportunity to avoid substantial fines and reinforce trust with customers and regulators, ultimately protecting NatWest's reputation and bottom line.

needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia audits the subscription flow, drafts the renewal-reminder template (24-hour rule), and rewrites the cancel page to meet CMA Part 4 wording.
needs_review.layman_explanation: If you charge customers on a subscription you must send pre-renewal reminders and let them cancel as easily as they signed up. CMA can fine up to £300k or 10% global turnover. From April 2025 these are direct CMA fines, no court order needed.
needs_review.enforcement_example: CMA under the DMCC Act 2024 (in force 6 April 2025): direct fines up to 10% of global turnover plus personal penalties up to GBP 300,000.

needs_review.fact: Investment, crypto and high-risk financial promotions must carry an FCA-compliant risk warning.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia confirms whether s.21 applies, sources an FCA-approved promoter (or routes via an existing authorised firm), and drafts compliant copy with the wording and prominence the FCA expects.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia confirms whether s.21 applies, sources an FCA-approved promoter (or routes via an existing authorised firm), and drafts compliant copy with the wording and prominence the FCA expects.
needs_review.layman_explanation: If you promote any kind of investment or crypto to UK consumers without the FCA-mandated risk warning and authorisation, that is a criminal offence under s.21 FSMA. Two years prison + unlimited fine.
needs_review.enforcement_example: FCA: criminal and civil sanctions for unauthorised financial promotions (s21 FSMA), including unlimited fines and public censure.

needs_review.fact: Consent signal detected (OneTrust), but 1 non-essential tracker(s) are present and may fire before opt-in: Hotjar (sets _hjHasCachedUserAttributes, _hjid, hj_visitor — Analytics, controller Hotjar).
needs_review.citation: UK PECR + UK GDPR + EU GDPR + ePrivacy · consent functionality
needs_review.evidence: homepage HTML · consent present + 1 non-essential trackers · verify pre-consent blocking
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia configures the consent platform to block these tags until opt-in and adds an equally prominent, functional Reject-All.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia configures the consent platform to block these tags until opt-in and adds an equally prominent, functional Reject-All.
needs_review.layman_explanation: A banner alone is not compliance. Under the ICO's 2025 standard a Reject-All option must be as prominent and functional as Accept-All, and non-essential tags (Hotjar) must not load until consent. The SHEIN and American Express cases both involved cookies set despite a Reject-All click. Verify these tags are genuinely blocked pre-consent.
needs_review.enforcement_example: ICO under PECR: enforcement notices and fines up to GBP 500,000, plus GDPR-level fines where consent is the lawful basis; the ICO has run cookie-consent sweeps against major UK sites.

regimes.why: serves EU clients (applies regardless of where the firm is registered)
applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: natwest is registered in the United Kingdom and its own website shows it serves clients in the UK, the EU and globally. It is therefore bound by UK law (UK GDPR, PECR, CMA/DMCC, sector regulators), EU law (GDPR, ePrivacy, the European Accessibility Act, DSA) and multiple international regimes, not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.
